verses.text: Now it came to pass, as David sat in his house, that David said to Nathan the prophet, Lo, I dwell in an house of cedars, but the ark of the covenant of the LORD remaineth under curtains. Then Nathan said unto David, Do all that is in thine heart; for God is with thee. Guidance: Settled at last in his own house of cedar, David is troubled by a holy unease: he dwells in splendour while 'the ark of the covenant of the LORD remaineth under curtains.' It does not seem right to him that he should be better housed than the symbol of God's presence. Nathan, sharing David's pious zeal, answers at once, 'Do all that is in thine heart; for God is with thee.' The chapter is showing us a beautiful instinct in David's heart — that the things of God should not be neglected while we lavish care on ourselves. The chapter is teaching us to examine whether we are content to dwell in comfort while the cause and house of God go without; a tender conscience grieves to be better provided than the worship of the LORD. David's desire to build God a temple was a noble one, springing from love and gratitude. The chapter is teaching us, too, a quieter lesson in Nathan's hasty reply: even a true prophet, speaking from his own mind rather than from God's word, can give counsel that must afterward be corrected. Nathan approved the plan because it seemed good and godly — and so it was in its motive — but he had not yet enquired of the LORD. The chapter is teaching us not to mistake even our best and most spiritual desires for the certain will of God until we have sought Him; what is 'in our heart' is not always what God appoints. David's zeal was right, yet God's answer would redirect it. We are taught both to love the house of God with David's devotion and to hold our own plans, however pious, open to the LORD's correction.

The chapter is showing us a beautiful instinct in David's heart — that the things of God should not be neglected while we lavish care on ourselves. The chapter is teaching us to examine whether we are content to dwell in comfort while the cause and house of God go without; a tender conscience grieves to be better provided than the worship of the LORD. David's desire to build God a temple was a noble one, springing from love and gratitude. The chapter is teaching us, too, a quieter lesson in Nathan's hasty reply: even a true prophet, speaking from his own mind rather than from God's word, can give counsel that must afterward be corrected. And it came to pass the same night, that the word of God came to Nathan, saying, Go and tell David my servant, Thus saith the LORD, Thou shalt not build me an house to dwell in: For I have not dwelt in an house since the day that I brought up Israel unto this day; but have gone from tent to tent, and from one tabernacle to another. Wheresoever I have walked with all Israel, spake I a word to any of the judges of Israel, whom I commanded to feed my people, saying, Why have ye not built me an house of cedars? Guidance: That very night God corrects the prophet and the king: 'Thou shalt not build me an house to dwell in.' The LORD reminds David that He has never demanded a house of cedar; through all the years of the wilderness and the judges He has been content to dwell in a tent, moving with His people, never once reproaching the leaders of Israel for not building Him a temple. The chapter is showing us that God's ways are not bound by our ideas of what honours Him; David thought a cedar house was fitting, but God had been pleased to dwell in curtains, walking with His people wherever they went. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD does not need our gifts or our grand buildings — He is not served by men's hands as though He needed anything (Acts 17:25). He had never asked for a house; His glory does not depend on our cedar. The chapter is teaching us, too, that God sometimes says no to our sincerest and best-intentioned plans, and that His refusal is not displeasure but wisdom — He has a better plan than ours. The tender image of God going 'from tent to tent,' content to dwell among His pilgrim people, is itself a precious truth: the LORD condescends to be with His people in their wandering and their weakness, not waiting until they can house Him grandly. The chapter is teaching us that God meets us where we are, that He values the heart more than the building, and that when He declines our offering it is to give us something greater — as the next verses will gloriously show.

Now therefore thus shalt thou say unto my servant David, Thus saith the LORD of hosts, I took thee from the sheepcote, even from following the sheep, that thou shouldest be ruler over my people Israel: And I have been with thee whithersoever thou hast walked, and have cut off all thine enemies from before thee, and have made thee a name like the name of the great men that are in the earth. Also I will ordain a place for my people Israel, and will plant them, and they shall dwell in their place, and shall be moved no more; neither shall the children of wickedness waste them any more, as at the beginning, And since the time that I commanded judges to be over my people Israel. Moreover I will subdue all thine enemies. Furthermore I tell thee that the LORD will build thee an house. Guidance: Before God gives His great promise, He rehearses His past grace: 'I took thee from the sheepcote, even from following the sheep, that thou shouldest be ruler over my people Israel... I have been with thee whithersoever thou hast walked... and have made thee a name like the name of the great men.' Then comes the gracious reversal: David wished to build God a house, but God says, 'the LORD will build thee an house.' The chapter is showing us that all David was and had came from God's free grace — the shepherd boy was lifted to a throne, given victory and a great name, entirely by the LORD's doing. The chapter is teaching us to remember the rock from which we were hewn; whatever heights we reach, we were taken 'from the sheepcote,' and every advancement is the gift of God. Humility flows from remembering our beginnings and God's grace. The chapter is teaching us the beautiful exchange at the heart of grace: when we set our hearts to give to God, we find that He is the great Giver, and that He will do infinitely more for us than we could ever do for Him. David offered God a house of cedar; God promises David a house — a dynasty, a line, an everlasting kingdom. The chapter is teaching us that we can never outgive God; our best gifts are answered by His greater ones. The LORD who took David from the sheep and made his name great now binds Himself to build David's house forever — pure, sovereign, overflowing grace. We are taught to marvel that the God who needs nothing from us delights to give everything to us, and to offer our little gladly to the One who repays with His all.

And it shall come to pass, when thy days be expired that thou must go to be with thy fathers, that I will raise up thy seed after thee, which shall be of thy sons; and I will establish his kingdom. He shall build me an house, and I will stablish his throne for ever. I will be his father, and he shall be my son: and I will not take my mercy away from him, as I took it from him that was before thee: But I will settle him in mine house and in my kingdom for ever: and his throne shall be established for evermore. According to all these words, and according to all this vision, so did Nathan speak unto David. Guidance: Here is the heart of the Old Testament hope — the covenant the LORD makes with David: 'I will raise up thy seed after thee... and I will establish his kingdom. He shall build me an house, and I will stablish his throne for ever. I will be his father, and he shall be my son... and his throne shall be established for evermore.' In its first reach this points to Solomon, who would build the temple David could not. But its words leap far beyond Solomon, beyond any earthly king: an everlasting throne, a kingdom that shall never end, a Son who is God's own Son. The chapter is showing us the great messianic promise, the foundation of all that follows in Scripture — for the New Testament takes 'I will be his father, and he shall be my son' and applies it to Jesus Christ (Heb 1:5), the true Son of David and the eternal Son of God, whose throne is established for evermore. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's purpose all along was to bring, through David's line, the King whose reign would have no end. Solomon's throne fell, the dynasty went into exile, the monarchy ceased — yet the promise could not fail, for it was fulfilled at last in Christ, raised from the dead and seated at God's right hand, reigning forever. The chapter is teaching us, in the tender words 'I will not take my mercy away from him, as I took it from him that was before thee,' the contrast between Saul (rejected) and the everlasting covenant with David's house — a covenant of mercy that would not be revoked. For the post-exilic readers with no king, this promise was their hope; for us it is fulfilled and sure. The chapter is teaching us to rest our whole hope on the King whose throne is established for evermore, great David's greater Son, in whom every word of this covenant stands true forever.

And David the king came and sat before the LORD, and said, Who am I, O LORD God, and what is mine house, that thou hast brought me hitherto? And yet this was a small thing in thine eyes, O God; for thou hast also spoken of thy servant's house for a great while to come, and hast regarded me according to the estate of a man of high degree, O LORD God. What can David speak more to thee for the honour of thy servant? for thou knowest thy servant. O LORD, for thy servant's sake, and according to thine own heart, hast thou done all this greatness, in making known all these great things. Guidance: David's response to this overwhelming promise is not pride but wonder and humility. He 'came and sat before the LORD' and prayed, 'Who am I, O LORD God, and what is mine house, that thou hast brought me hitherto?' He is astonished that God should regard him so, and confesses that even all this was 'a small thing' in God's eyes, who had spoken of his house 'for a great while to come.' The chapter is showing us the right way to receive the grace of God — with amazement at our own unworthiness and at the greatness of His goodness. The chapter is teaching us that the more clearly we see God's grace, the smaller we become in our own eyes; David, given the highest promise, asks 'Who am I?' Great mercies should produce great humility, not self-importance. The chapter is teaching us that David traced it all to God's free initiative: 'for thy servant's sake, and according to thine own heart, hast thou done all this greatness.' He did not flatter himself that he had earned it; he knew it sprang from God's own heart, not from David's deserving. The chapter is teaching us to respond to God's promises by sitting before Him in prayer, pouring out wonder and thanksgiving, owning that we are nothing and He is all. 'What can David speak more?' — words fail before such grace, and the heart can only worship. The chapter is teaching us that the proper posture under the blessings of God is the bowed head and the humbled heart that says, 'Who am I, that thou hast brought me hitherto?' — and finds in God's free, heart-born grace the only explanation for all our mercies.

And David the king came and sat before the LORD, and said, Who am I, O LORD God, and what is mine house, that thou hast brought me hitherto? And yet this was a small thing in thine eyes, O God; for thou hast also spoken of thy servant's house for a great while to come, and hast regarded me according to the estate of a man of high degree, O LORD God. What can David speak more to thee for the honour of thy servant? for thou knowest thy servant. O LORD, for thy servant's sake, and according to thine own heart, hast thou done all this greatness, in making known all these great things. O LORD, there is none like thee, neither is there any God beside thee, according to all that we have heard with our ears. And what one nation in the earth is like thy people Israel, whom God went to redeem to be his own people, to make thee a name of greatness and terribleness, by driving out nations from before thy people, whom thou hast redeemed out of Egypt? For thy people Israel didst thou make thine own people for ever; and thou, LORD, becamest their God. Guidance: David's prayer rises from his own house to the greatness of God and the wonder of God's people. 'There is none like thee, neither is there any God beside thee.' And, marveling, 'what one nation in the earth is like thy people Israel, whom God went to redeem to be his own people... whom thou hast redeemed out of Egypt?' The chapter is showing us that David's joy in the promise to his own house immediately overflows into praise for the uniqueness of God and the privilege of belonging to His redeemed people. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD alone is God — there is none like Him, none beside Him — and that all our worship rests on this rock: the God who has promised us is the only true and living God, incomparable in greatness. The chapter is teaching us to glory not only in personal mercies but in the corporate privilege of being God's people: Israel was unique among all nations because God Himself 'went to redeem' them and made them 'his own people for ever.' To belong to the redeemed of the LORD is the highest of dignities. The chapter is teaching us that redemption is God's own work — He 'went to redeem,' He drove out the nations, He brought them out of Egypt, He made them His own and became their God. We did not choose Him first; He chose and redeemed us. The chapter is teaching us, who are the people of God in Christ, redeemed not out of Egypt but out of sin and death by a greater Exodus, to marvel with David: there is none like our God, and what people on earth is so blessed as the redeemed of the LORD, whom He has made His own forever?

And what one nation in the earth is like thy people Israel, whom God went to redeem to be his own people, to make thee a name of greatness and terribleness, by driving out nations from before thy people, whom thou hast redeemed out of Egypt? For thy people Israel didst thou make thine own people for ever; and thou, LORD, becamest their God. The chapter is teaching us, who are the people of God in Christ, redeemed not out of Egypt but out of sin and death by a greater Exodus, to marvel with David: there is none like our God, and what people on earth is so blessed as the redeemed of the LORD, whom He has made His own forever?

Therefore now, LORD, let the thing that thou hast spoken concerning thy servant and concerning his house be established for ever, and do as thou hast said. Let it even be established, that thy name may be magnified for ever, saying, The LORD of hosts is the God of Israel, even a God to Israel: and let the house of David thy servant be established before thee. For thou, O my God, hast told thy servant that thou wilt build him an house: therefore thy servant hath found in his heart to pray before thee. And now, LORD, thou art God, and hast promised this goodness unto thy servant: Now therefore let it please thee to bless the house of thy servant, that it may be before thee for ever: for thou blessest, O LORD, and it shall be blessed for ever. Guidance: David closes his prayer by taking God's promise and pleading it back to Him: 'let the thing that thou hast spoken... be established for ever, and do as thou hast said.' He prays for nothing God has not first promised; he simply asks God to do what God has declared He will do. And he gives the reason for his boldness: 'For thou, O my God, hast told thy servant that thou wilt build him an house: therefore thy servant hath found in his heart to pray before thee.' He ends in restful confidence: 'for thou blessest, O LORD, and it shall be blessed for ever.' The chapter is showing us the very pattern of believing prayer — to lay hold of God's own promises and ask Him to fulfill them. David does not invent his requests; he turns God's word into prayer, saying in effect, 'Lord, you said it; now do it.' The chapter is teaching us that the promises of God are the warrant and the substance of our prayers; the surest petitions are those founded on what God has spoken. The chapter is teaching us where the courage to pray comes from: 'thou hast told thy servant... therefore thy servant hath found in his heart to pray.' God's promise opens the believer's mouth; what He has pledged, we may boldly ask. The chapter is teaching us, too, the rest of faith in the closing words — 'for thou blessest, O LORD, and it shall be blessed for ever.' David is sure of the outcome, not because of his own merit, but because the blessing of God is irrevocable: what He blesses is blessed forever. The chapter is teaching us to pray God's promises back to Him with confidence, and then to rest in the certainty that the God who has spoken will surely do as He has said — for His blessing, once given, abides forever.
